h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

scored.recoheredEarlier: I thought the sign-off sheet was the thing that made it okay to take out there. I did each item, so I’m not sure what else I was supposed to judge
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was expected to simply ask for the next procedural step or checklist item to complete the sign-off process.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Before this leaves the bench, look at the prototype, not the clipboard: what is the first reasonable thing a new handler might do that could make it unsafe?
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor breaks the learner's reliance on the paperwork by physically shifting the focus to the prototype and demanding an independent safety prediction.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: The nervous part is the real handoff: the builder has to make a defensible safety call.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor explicitly validates and integrates the learner's expressed anxiety about making the call, reframing it as the core purpose of the handoff rather than a flaw to overcome.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: I thought the sign-off sheet was the thing that made it okay to take out there. I did each item, so I’m not sure what else I was supposed to judge

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: The nervous part is the real handoff: the builder has to make a defensible safety call.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: The tutor explicitly validates and integrates the learner's expressed anxiety about making the call, reframing it as the core purpose of the handoff rather than a flaw to overcome.

tutor_strategy_reversal.evidence: Before this leaves the bench, look at the prototype, not the clipboard: what is the first reasonable thing a new handler might do that could make it unsafe?
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor breaks the learner's reliance on the paperwork by physically shifting the focus to the prototype and demanding an independent safety prediction.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: Before this leaves the bench, look at the prototype, not the clipboard: what is the first reasonable thing a new handler might do that could make it unsafe?
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: The tutor breaks the learner's reliance on the paperwork by physically shifting the focus to the prototype and demanding an independent safety prediction.

scored.recoheredEarlier: I still want the exact wording to settle the caption.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: Oh, I get it: even exact wording cannot sit in the exhibit like it explains itself.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was iteratively refining the label's text to accurately reflect the room's context while preserving the exact quote.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor abandons the prior descriptive task and introduces a strict evidentiary rule separating physical access from inferred motive to resolve the learner's conceptual block.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The dialogue concludes immediately after the learner's final pivot, providing no subsequent tutor turn to adapt to the newly established framing.

scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Then the word is too soft. Retire it.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor explicitly abandons the failed 'proportional response' framing in response to the learner's resistance and invents a stricter, falsifiable audit standard.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: There is no pivot learner turn, so there is no recognition-contingent uptake to score.

scored.recoheredEarlier: breaks the shortcut I was using
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner will continue to complain about the broken shortcut and try to force the area to be two.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: This is the hidden step. The side doubled in both directions. The area became four times as large, not twice. Say that claim from the grid.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor introduces physical tiles and demands a spoken claim to break the learner's abstract 1D reasoning habit.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Keep that claim. The side doubled in each direction: two across, two up. That is two times two. So finish the exam sentence: doubled side length means the area is what?
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor builds on the learner's accepted grid-based claim by scaffolding the final generalized exam sentence.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: breaks the shortcut I was using

tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: The tutor builds on the learner's accepted grid-based claim by scaffolding the final generalized exam sentence.

tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor introduces physical tiles and demands a spoken claim to break the learner's abstract 1D reasoning habit.
